article_title: Tuberculous meningitis with dementia as the presenting symptom after intramedullary spinal cord tumor resection
label: Fig. 1
caption: (a) T2 MRI and (b) Gd-enhanced MRI before the initial surgery (nine years ago), showing the presence of an intramedullary tumor.

article_title: Tuberculous meningitis with dementia as the presenting symptom after intramedullary spinal cord tumor resection
label: Fig. 2
caption: (a) T2 MRI after the first surgery, showing total resection of the tumor. (b) T2 MRI four years after the first surgery, showing regrowth of the tumor. (c) T2 MRI and (d) Gd enhanced MRI nine years after the first surgery.

article_title: Tuberculous meningitis with dementia as the presenting symptom after intramedullary spinal cord tumor resection
label: Fig. 3
caption: (a) Normal chest X-ray. (b) Brain CT showing slight ventricle enlargement. (c) Brain MRI showing ventricle enlargement.

article_title: Anterior intradural CSF collection causing postoperative neurological deterioration after intramedullary tumor resection and associated syringomyelia: illustrative case
label: FIG. 1.
caption: Preoperative cervical spine MR images demonstrating an intramedullary mass extending from C6 to T2. A: T2-weighted image showing an associated syringomyelia with cranial extension of the syrinx to the C1–2 level (arrow). B: Contrast-enhanced T1-weighted image demonstrating a contrast-enhancing intramedullary mass (arrowheads).

article_title: Anterior intradural CSF collection causing postoperative neurological deterioration after intramedullary tumor resection and associated syringomyelia: illustrative case
label: FIG. 2.
caption: Cervical spine MR images demonstrating an anterior intradural fluid collection resulting in dorsal displacement of the spinal cord (arrow). A and B: T2-weighted images showing a hyperintense anterior intradural collection (asterisk) with signal intensity distinct from CSF. C: T1-weighted image demonstrating low signal intensity of the collection.

article_title: Anterior intradural CSF collection causing postoperative neurological deterioration after intramedullary tumor resection and associated syringomyelia: illustrative case
label: FIG. 3.
caption: Intraoperative findings and ultrasound images. A: Intraoperative ultrasound demonstrating a prominent anterior CSF space compressing the spinal cord posteriorly, consistent with a localized intradural CSFoma (asterisk). B: Intraoperative microscopic view revealing a markedly thinned segment of the spinal cord, through which the ventral aspect of the cord is visible, raising the possibility of a minute communication with the anterior intradural space (arrow). C: Expansive duraplasty was performed using an artificial dural substitute to create adequate intradural space and restore CSF flow. D: Intraoperative ultrasound image obtained after duraplasty demonstrating adequate decompression of the spinal cord with expansion of the intradural space created by the artificial dural substitute.

article_title: Anterior intradural CSF collection causing postoperative neurological deterioration after intramedullary tumor resection and associated syringomyelia: illustrative case
label: FIG. 4.
caption: Postoperative cervical spine MR image demonstrating restoration of the spinal cord to its normal position (arrow) with a marked reduction in the associated syrinx (arrowheads) compared with the preoperative images.

article_title: Primary thoracic intramedullary spinal cord tumor with likely metastases of glial origin to the lumbosacral vertebrae: Illustrative case
label: Figure 1:
caption: (a and b) Preoperative images – T1 pre- and postcontrast of thoracic spine and (c) one year postoperative – T1 postcontrast of thoracic spine.

label: Figure 2:
caption: (a) 18 months postoperatively – T2 noncontrast of thoracic spine, (b) 20 months postoperatively – T1 with contrast of thoracic spine, and 3 years postoperatively showing progression (c) 3 years postoperatively – T1 with contrast of cervicothoracic spine.

article_title: Primary thoracic intramedullary spinal cord tumor with likely metastases of glial origin to the lumbosacral vertebrae: Illustrative case
label: Figure 3:
caption: (a) Three years postoperatively – T1 with the contrast of cervical spine, (b) 3 years postoperatively – T1 with the contrast of lumbar spine, and (c) X-ray-guided biopsy of L4 vertebral body.
